title: Hormuz Blockade Reshapes Global Oil Flows as US and China Coordinate Crisis Response
subhead: The Iran war's closure of the Strait of Hormuz has created history's largest oil supply disruption. China and the US are managing the shock through coordinated import cuts and export surges, while Gulf states accelerate pipeline projects to bypass the chokepoint permanently.

sections.heading: Hormuz Closure Creates Largest Oil Supply Disruption in History
sections.body:
The Iran-US conflict has produced the largest oil supply disruption in history, with roughly 10 million barrels per day cut from global markets—equivalent to 10% of total world consumption, according to the International Energy Agency. The Strait of Hormuz, which normally sees about 60 vessel transits daily, has collapsed to approximately six ships per day.

Despite the magnitude of the disruption, Brent crude closed Thursday at $97.91 per barrel—lower than the peaks seen during the smaller 2022 Russian invasion shock. The relative containment reflects aggressive coordination between the US and China, which together account for the adjustment of 7.1 million bpd—roughly 70% of the lost Gulf exports.

US non-Middle East exports have surged by 3.5 million bpd, while China has cut imports by 3.6 million bpd—equivalent to Japan's entire daily consumption. Japan, South Korea, and India have collectively reduced imports by another 3.6 million bpd.
sections.whyItMatters: The coordinated response from Washington and Beijing demonstrates that the two largest oil market players can stabilize prices during a severe crisis, but the sustainability of this arrangement depends on inventory drawdowns that cannot continue indefinitely.

sections.heading: Trump-Xi Summit Yields Energy Cooperation Amid War
sections.body:
President Donald Trump met with President Xi Jinping in Beijing this week, with both leaders agreeing that the Strait of Hormuz must reopen for the free flow of energy. The summit produced an understanding that China will purchase more US oil—a move Energy Secretary Chris Wright called a "natural energy trade" between the world's largest producer and largest importer.

China's Middle East oil imports have been largely cut off by Iran's blockade. Beijing is drawing down its 1.4 billion barrel strategic reserve—the world's largest—to weather the disruption. Morgan Stanley estimates China can sustain current drawdown rates for months, potentially through year-end.

Iran is selectively allowing Chinese vessels through Hormuz following diplomatic outreach, according to Iranian state media. Trump told Fox News that China had agreed to buy US oil and send ships to Texas, Louisiana, and Alaska, though Beijing has not publicly confirmed any specific purchase agreements.
sections.whyItMatters: The US-China energy coordination during the Iran crisis represents a rare area of great power cooperation, but divergent interests remain: China needs supply security while the US seeks market access and leverage over Iran.

sections.heading: UAE Fast-Tracks Pipeline to Bypass Hormuz Permanently
sections.body:
Abu Dhabi is accelerating construction of a second West-East pipeline to Fujairah, expected online by 2027, which would double ADNOC's export capacity through routes that avoid the Strait of Hormuz entirely. The existing Habshan-Fujairah pipeline can carry up to 1.8 million barrels per day; the new project would bring total bypass capacity to roughly 3.6 million bpd.

The UAE is currently producing between 1.8 and 2.1 million bpd—down from pre-war levels above 3 million—due to export constraints and infrastructure attacks. ADNOC has targeted capacity of 4.9 million bpd, though reaching that goal now depends on how quickly bypass infrastructure can be built.

Energy Secretary Wright said Hormuz "will lose its importance" as Gulf states build alternative routes. "This is a card you can play once," he said of Iran's blockade strategy.
sections.whyItMatters: The UAE's infrastructure push signals that Gulf producers are treating the Hormuz threat as permanent rather than temporary. The pipeline investments will reshape export routes regardless of how the war ends.

sections.heading: UAE Exits OPEC as Cartel Faces Existential Crisis
sections.body:
The United Arab Emirates has announced its departure from OPEC after 59 years of membership. The move comes as the Iran war has exposed the cartel's inability to manage supply during a Gulf security crisis. The UAE and Saudi Arabia are now the only major Gulf producers able to export significant volumes during the Hormuz closure.

OPEC has also cut its 2026 global oil demand growth forecast to about 1.2 million barrels per day, joining the IEA in downward revisions attributed to the Iran war's economic impact. OPEC+ plans gradual quota increases to restore 1.65 million bpd of previously halted production by September—but those additional barrels remain trapped behind the Hormuz blockade.

The UAE exit follows years of tension over production quotas and the group's Saudi-dominated decision-making. Abu Dhabi has invested heavily in capacity expansion that OPEC limits prevented it from fully utilizing.
sections.whyItMatters: The UAE departure weakens OPEC at a moment when the cartel's crisis-response credibility is already damaged. A smaller, less cohesive OPEC+ changes the dynamics of global oil governance.

sections.heading: Piracy Returns Off Somalia as Shipping Diverts Around Africa
sections.body:
Global shipping faces a fresh security threat as piracy has resurfaced off Somalia, with three ships hijacked in recent weeks. The resurgence comes as vessels divert around the Cape of Good Hope to avoid both the Red Sea (Houthi attacks) and Hormuz—adding two to three weeks and approximately $1 million in fuel costs per voyage.

The tankers Honour 25 and Eureka, along with the cargo ship Sward, remain under pirate control as of May 8. Organized crime groups in Puntland are exploiting the diversion of international naval patrols to the Gulf and Red Sea. Pirates are using seized dhows as mother ships to extend their operational range.

The EU's Operation Atalanta and Combined Task Force 151 continue patrols, but the vast area and stretched resources make full coverage impossible. Industry experts warn that any major piracy resurgence could add billions in costs—during the 2011 crisis, hijackings cost an estimated $7 billion annually in military operations, rerouting, security, and insurance.
sections.whyItMatters: The convergence of Hormuz, Red Sea, and now Somali piracy creates a three-front maritime security crisis that is reshaping global trade routes and pushing shipping costs higher across all sectors.

sections.heading: Iran Targets Undersea Cables as Digital Chokepoints Emerge
sections.body:
Iranian state-linked media have floated plans to charge operators of undersea internet cables in the Strait of Hormuz for access to what Tehran claims as its offshore territory. The threat exposes the vulnerability of the 500+ submarine cables carrying more than 95% of international data traffic.

Several critical cable routes run through Middle East maritime chokepoints: the Red Sea, Bab el-Mandeb, Suez Canal, and Hormuz. A 2024 incident in the Red Sea disrupted 25% of Europe-Asia internet traffic. Iranian warnings that cables are a "vulnerable point" for Gulf economies signal that digital infrastructure has become a legitimate target in modern conflict.

Deliberate cable damage would fragment global connectivity, disrupt financial markets, and degrade military communications. Repair in contested waters requires international coordination that may be impossible during active conflict.
sections.whyItMatters: The Iran conflict is expanding beyond physical energy infrastructure to digital infrastructure. Undersea cables represent a new class of chokepoint where disruption causes cascading economic and security effects.

sections.heading: India-UAE Deepen Energy Ties Amid Hormuz Threat
sections.body:
Prime Minister Narendra Modi visited Abu Dhabi this week, signing agreements on strategic petroleum reserves and LPG supply that experts call "game-changing" for India's energy security. The UAE committed $5 billion in investment covering energy, defense, infrastructure, and advanced technology.

India's crude basket price jumped from $69 per barrel in February to over $113 in March, forcing emergency measures and supply diversification to Russia, Venezuela, and West Africa. The new agreements include framework for a strategic defense partnership focused on maritime security and cyber defense.

ADNOC signed strategic agreements with Indian partners on fuel storage, supply, and trading cooperation. India has already crossed 50% non-fossil installed electricity capacity ahead of schedule, which has helped limit the crisis impact on its power sector despite heavy oil dependence in transportation.
sections.whyItMatters: India's UAE partnership represents a hedge against Hormuz vulnerability, with strategic storage, supply diversification, and defense cooperation creating a multi-layered response to Gulf supply risk.

sections.heading: Russia Sanctions Falter as Urals Trades Above Price Cap
sections.body:
Western sanctions targeting Russian oil exports have reached an impasse. Russian Urals crude is now trading above $80 per barrel FOB—nearly double the G7 price cap of $44.20. European plans to replace the price cap with a full maritime services ban appear unlikely to succeed without US support.

The so-called "shadow fleet" continues handling the bulk of Russian crude exports, but Greek shippers now cover up to one-third of loadings from Baltic and Black Sea ports—suggesting conventional shipping is increasingly willing to test enforcement limits. Ukraine has doubled its drone strikes on Russian oil refineries this year, adding pressure on Moscow's downstream capacity.

The sanctions regime's weakening coincides with the Iran crisis, which has absorbed Western attention and diverted naval assets from sanctions enforcement to Gulf operations.
sections.whyItMatters: The Russia sanctions architecture is eroding at exactly the moment when Western enforcement capacity is stretched thin by the Iran conflict. Higher Russian revenues complicate any diplomatic path to ending the Ukraine war.

bottomLine: The Iran war has produced history's largest oil supply shock, but prices remain contained at $98/bbl because China and the US are actively managing the crisis through import cuts, export surges, and inventory drawdowns. This cooperation is fragile—US strategic reserves are under pressure, and China cannot draw down stocks forever. Meanwhile, Gulf states are treating Hormuz vulnerability as permanent, with the UAE fast-tracking pipelines that will reshape export routes regardless of how the war ends. The conflict has also exposed new vulnerabilities: undersea cables are now explicit targets, and piracy has returned to Somali waters as shipping diverts around Africa. The energy geopolitics of 2026 are being rewritten in real time.
sources: IEA Oil Market Report May 2026, CNBC, Reuters, Energy Intelligence, Morgan Stanley, Deutsche Bank, Deutsche Welle, Cyprus Mail, Time, The Conversation, OilPrice.com, Bloomberg, IEA, EIA
